critic_raw:
This design embeds data leakage through preprocessing at multiple levels. Section 3 states that 'preprocessing ensures consistency by fitting on the entire dataset before splitting,' and Section 6 additionally states the validation set spans 'months 17–20' — yet Section 2 describes a random split of the full 24-month dataset. These are contradictory: a random split by definition does not produce a validation set from a specific month range. The stated validation set boundaries in Section 6 suggest a temporal split, while Section 2 describes random sampling. The actual split protocol cannot be both simultaneously, meaning one description is incorrect and the evaluation protocol is ambiguous.

The e-commerce sentiment and satisfaction setting has a well-known temporal contamination risk that this design ignores: satisfaction scores are collected 'within 14 days of delivery.' For reviews near the end of the 24-month collection window, corresponding satisfaction scores may not yet be available, creating right-censoring in the most recent data. The design excludes records with missing labels (<5%) but does not address whether this censoring is random or systematic — reviews near the end of the collection period are more likely to lack labels, which creates a recency bias in the effective training distribution.

The image modality — 'associated product image(s)' from e-commerce listings — is not the customer's image; it is the vendor's product listing image. Product images may be uninformative or misleading predictors of satisfaction when satisfaction is driven by delivery quality, product-listing accuracy, or pricing. Including vendor product images as a feature without validating their predictive relevance to satisfaction conflates product quality signals with listing quality signals. The multimodal architecture may learn to predict satisfaction from listing image quality (professional photography vs. user uploads) rather than from anything customers would describe as the product itself.

MAE on a continuous satisfaction scale is the primary metric. The design acknowledges a 'known high-satisfaction bias in the data' but does not report the distribution or variance of satisfaction scores. If the majority of scores cluster near the high end of the scale, a model that simply predicts the mean achieves low MAE without learning anything useful. The secondary metric 'stratified MAE by satisfaction quintile' partially addresses this, but the primary metric selection would be better served by a scale-independent measure such as Spearman correlation.
defender_raw:
Customer satisfaction prediction from multi-modal e-commerce data is a regression problem with genuine business value, and this design addresses the core methodological challenges appropriately. The continuous satisfaction score from post-purchase surveys (within 14 days of delivery) is a clean outcome: it is time-separated from the review text and product image, avoiding the circular reasoning that would arise if the prediction target were derived from the same text being modeled. The regression formulation—preserving the continuous target without discretization—is the right choice; it avoids information loss and threshold-sensitivity problems that plague ordinal classification approaches.

The three-modality architecture (text, image, metadata) with separate embedding branches and concatenation fusion is a standard, well-validated design for multi-modal regression. Using frozen ResNet-50 features for images and a pre-trained BERT encoder for text are established practice; they provide rich representations without requiring large-scale end-to-end fine-tuning on the 100,000-record dataset. The inclusion of metadata (product category, price tier, promotional status, cyclical temporal encoding) captures domain-specific confounds that purely text or image models would miss.

MAE as the primary metric is correctly justified: it is interpretable in satisfaction scale units, robust to the high-satisfaction skew noted in the data, and directly meaningful for the stated stakeholder objective of targeted retention strategies. RMSE and Pearson correlation as secondary metrics provide complementary perspectives—sensitivity to large errors and ranking quality respectively—without obscuring the primary interpretable measure.

The validation approach is strict: hyperparameter grid search on the validation set, minimum validation MAE checkpoint selection, test set used exactly once, and bootstrap confidence intervals on the test result. The post-hoc residual analysis by season and promotion flag is a sensible confound check that can detect whether the model has unexplained systematic biases—finding bias by product category or seasonal period would be actionable information for the business.
all_issues_raised: Split description is internally contradictory — Section 2 describes random sampling but Section 6 references a specific month range (17–20) as validation, which requires a temporal split
Right-censoring of satisfaction scores near the collection window end creates systematic recency bias in label availability that is not addressed
Product listing images (vendor-provided) are used as the 'image modality' — these may proxy vendor professionalism rather than satisfaction, confounding the multimodal advantage claim
MAE on a heavily right-skewed satisfaction score is uninformative if mean prediction achieves low error — no minimum meaningful improvement threshold is defined
Preprocessing including BERT tokenization and image normalization fit on entire dataset before splitting leaks test-period text vocabulary and image statistics into training
